explanation: Leaves row-count chatter enabled, increasing client and network noise.
Allows some run-time errors to leave a transaction partially applied.
Couples callers to every current and future column of Sales.Orders.
Returns an arbitrary set of rows because no ordering is specified.
Uses a duplicate-eliminating operation where OrderID is already unique.
Can return uncommitted, missing, or duplicated rows.
YEAR on the indexed date column prevents a simple range seek.
Compares an int column with nvarchar input and can force conversion.
OR-based optional filtering makes cardinality estimation and seeking difficult.
A leading wildcard prevents a normal index seek on Comments.
NOT IN has surprising results when the subquery can produce NULL.
Wrapping CustomerID in ISNULL makes the predicate non-sargable.
Arithmetic on OrderID prevents direct index matching.
Inequality often scans broad portions of the table.
Omits a join predicate and multiplies Orders by Customers.
Comma joins obscure join predicates and make accidental cartesian joins easier.
Executes a scalar lookup for every qualifying order row.
Runs a correlated EXISTS check per outer row instead of expressing a set join.
Repeats the same CASE expression in select and ordering logic.
Ordering by a column position becomes fragile when the select list changes.
OFFSET pagination has no stable tie breaker.
Performs needless duplicate removal between disjoint literal branches.
Counts all matching rows when only existence is needed.
SELECT assignment silently accepts multiple rows and keeps the last value.
A table variable may receive poor cardinality estimates on older compatibility levels.
Creates a temp-table schema implicitly instead of declaring it explicitly.
Uses a cursor for work that can be expressed set-wise.
Performs iterative work instead of a set-based operation.
Concatenates a value into executable SQL text.
Uses sp_executesql but still embeds the value rather than binding it.
Leaves an explicitly created temporary object until procedure scope ends.
Captures an error but neither rethrows nor returns a failure status.
Uses RAISERROR rather than THROW for new error paths.
PRINT is buffered and unsuitable for structured diagnostics.
Embeds a business identifier instead of accepting it as input or looking it up.
Embeds an unlabelled date constant in query logic.
Relies on session-sensitive date string conversion.
Applies COALESCE in a join predicate, inhibiting normal join optimization.
Self-joins Orders solely to retrieve the same key.
Sorts every order by an unselective text column.
Aggregates the entire fact-like order-line table for a single scalar result.
Applies a row-level predicate after grouping rather than in WHERE.
Orders a one-row aggregate result unnecessarily.
MERGE has documented concurrency and correctness edge cases.
Issues a write that changes no data and still causes logging and locking.
Deletes based on a broad business value rather than a stable key.
Intentionally blocks a worker instead of using asynchronous scheduling.
Starts a transaction outside an error-handling boundary.
Changes session state and introduces fragile identity-management coupling.
Invokes a system routine without schema qualification.
Changes a session setting and does not restore it.
